8: ho laos houtos tois cheilesin me tima, he de kardia auton porro apechei ap emou — this people honors me with the lips but their heart is far from me — Matthew cites Isaiah 29:13 LXX as the proof-text for the Pharisees' tradition-over-Torah priority. Kardia (heart) in the Jewish anthropology is the seat of will, understanding, and intention — not merely emotion; far-heartedness is not coldness of feeling but absence of genuine covenantal alignment. matēn de sebontai me — in vain do they worship me — maten (vainly, to no purpose) makes the worship not merely imperfect but empty, producing nothing that constitutes true worship.
1: tote proserchontai tō Iēsou apo Ierosolymōn Pharisaioi kai grammateis — then Pharisees and scribes come to Jesus from Jerusalem (apo Ierosolymōn: from Jerusalem — this is not a local Galilean confrontation but a delegation from the religious capital; the phrase signals official, institutional opposition): the Jerusalem origin elevates the confrontation; these are not provincial critics but representatives of the establishment. The timing ("then") connects the purity controversy to the Gentile expansion hinted at in the feeding of Matthew 14.
2: dia ti hoi mathētai sou parabainousin tēn paradosin tōn presbyterōn — why do your disciples transgress the tradition of the elders (paradosis tōn presbyterōn: the tradition of the elders — paradosis, that which is handed down, a transmitted tradition; presbyterōn, elders: the oral tradition attributed to revered predecessors, not the Torah itself) — ou gar niptontai tas cheiras autōn hotan arton esthiōsin (for they do not wash their hands when they eat bread): the charge is violation of oral-traditional purity rules, not a Mosaic command. The handwashing regulation is Pharisaic extension, not Sinai legislation.
3: dia ti kai hymeis parabainete tēn entolēn tou theou dia tēn paradosin hymōn — why do you also transgress the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition? — Jesus's counter-accusation inverts the charge: the Pharisees accuse the disciples of breaking human tradition; Jesus accuses the Pharisees of breaking divine commandment. Dia tēn paradosin hymōn (for the sake of your tradition — dia with the accusative: on account of, motivated by): tradition is the cause; the violated commandment is the effect. The Corban example (Matthew 15:4-6) is the evidence.
4: ho gar theos eipen tima ton patera kai tēn mētera — for God said: honor your father and mother (Exodus 20:12; Deuteronomy 5:16) — kai ho kakologōn patera ē mētera thanatō teleutatō (and whoever speaks evil of father or mother, let them die — kakologōn present participle of kakologeō: to speak evil, to curse; thanatō teleutatō: let them die by death — the intensive idiom from Exodus 21:17): the two commandments are placed together as a unit — the Fifth Commandment (honor) and the death penalty for its inversion (cursing). The positive and negative frame the absolute importance God attaches to parental honor.
5: hymeis de legete hos an eipē tō patri ē tē mētri — but you say: whoever says to his father or mother — dōron ho ean ex emou ōphelēthēs (a gift — whatever you would have received from me is Corban/devoted to God — dōron: a gift, an offering): the Corban formula (korban in Mark 7:11, Aramaic for dedicated offering) allowed a person to declare property "devoted to God" as a vow, thereby making it legally unavailable to parents even while the person retained practical use of it. The declaration was a legal evasion of the Fifth Commandment's financial obligations.
6: kai ou mē timēsei ton patera autou — and he will not honor his father (ou mē + subjunctive: emphatic negation — will certainly not honor him) — kai ēkyrōsate ton logon tou theou dia tēn paradosin hymōn (and you have nullified the word of God for the sake of your tradition — ēkyrōsate aorist of akyroō: to invalidate, make void — a legal term for annulling a binding decree): the accusation is precise: the oral tradition does not supplement but cancels the divine command. Ton logon tou theou (the word of God) is the same phrase used in 13:19 for the word of the kingdom.
7: hypokritai kalōs eprophēteusen peri hymōn Ēsaias — hypocrites, Isaiah prophesied well about you (hypokritai: actors, those who play a role while concealing their true identity — the theatrical word now established as Jesus's standard term for the Pharisees; cf. Matthew 6:2,5,16; Matthew 23:13-29) — kalōs eprophēteusen (prophesied well/rightly): the Isaiah citation is not a forced application but an exact match; Isaiah described them before they existed, which Jesus calls prophesying "well" (accurately, fittingly).
9: matēn de sebontai me didaskontes didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn — they worship me in vain, teaching as teachings commandments of men (matēn: in vain, for nothing, pointlessly — an adverb of futility; sebontai: they revere, worship — the worship is genuine in form but empty in content) — didaskalias entalmata anthrōpōn (commandments of men as teachings — entalma: a commandment, a prescribed rule; the phrase echoes Isaiah 29:13 LXX): the Corban ruling is not a marginal case but the symptom of a systemic substitution of human prescription for divine command.
10: kai proskalesamenos ton ochlon eipen autois — calling the crowd to him he said to them — akouete kai syniete (hear and understand — akouete present imperative: keep hearing; syniete present imperative: keep putting it together, keep understanding): the double imperative invites the crowd into the purity debate that the Pharisees framed as purely internal to the tradition. Jesus makes the issue public, democratizing what the scribal system had restricted to expert debate.
11: ou to eisercomenon eis to stoma koinoi ton anthrōpon — not what enters the mouth defiles the person (koinoi present of koinoō: to make common, to defile ritually — koinos means common in the sense of ordinary/unclean, not the sacral category) — alla to ekporeuomenon ek tou stomatos touto koinoi ton anthrōpon (but what goes out of the mouth, this defiles the person): the statement is revolutionary — it relocates the locus of defilement from external contact (food, handwashing) to internal production (speech, and by implication thought; Matthew 15:18-19 makes this explicit). Mark adds "thus he declared all foods clean" (Matthew 7:19).
12: tote proselthontes hoi mathētai legousin autō — then the disciples coming said to him — oidas hoti hoi Pharisaioi akousantes ton logon eskandalisthēsan (do you know that the Pharisees hearing the word were offended/caused to stumble — eskandalisthēsan aorist passive of skandalizō: were caused to stumble, took offense; the word carries the connotation of a fatal tripping, not mere irritation): the disciples are worried about the political fallout. Jesus's response (Matthew 15:13-14) does not address the politics but judges the Pharisees' spiritual root.
13: pasa phyteia hēn ouk ephyteusen ho patēr mou ho ouranios ekrizōthēsetai — every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be uprooted (phyteia: a planting, something planted — phyteuō: to plant; ekrizōthēsetai future passive of ekrizoō: to uproot, pull up by the roots): the imagery of planting and uprooting echoes the parable of the wheat and tares (Matthew 13:24-30) and the Baptist's warning (Matthew 3:10: "every tree not bearing good fruit is cut down"). The heavenly Father as the gardener determines what survives the final assessment.
14: aphete autous typhlos eisin hodēgoi — leave them; they are blind guides (typhlos hodēgoi: blind guides — the compound describes their double failure: they lead without sight) — typhlon de typhlos ean hodēgē (if blind leads blind — the two blindnesses in the chain) — amphoteroi eis bothynon pesountai (both will fall into a pit — bothynon: a hole in the ground, a well-pit): the Pharisees are not merely wrong but actively dangerous to their followers. The image was proverbial in antiquity; Jesus applies the proverb with precision to the situation of Israel's religious leadership.
15: apokritheis de ho Petros eipen autō phrason hēmin tēn parabolēn — Peter answering said to him: explain the parable to us (phrason aorist imperative of phrazō: to explain, point out, indicate clearly) — the request uses the parable-explanation vocabulary of Matthew 13:36, where the disciples asked for the weeds-parable explanation. Peter is asking for the private decoding the disciples received in Matthew 13. The disciples are still in the transitional state between public parable-hearing and private parable-understanding.
16: ho de eipen akmēn kai hymeis asyneti este — he said: are you still also without understanding (akmēn: still, even now — a classical Greek adverb marking continuation of a state; asyneti: without understanding — a- privative + synetos, the adjective from syniēmi: lacking the faculty of synthesis/comprehension): the gentle rebuke connects to the understanding-vocabulary of Matthew 13. The disciples, though privileged to receive explanations, are still not fully understanding.
17: ou noeite hoti pan to eisporeuomenon eis to stoma eis tēn koilian chōrei — do you not perceive that everything entering the mouth goes into the stomach (noeite present of noeō: to perceive, apprehend intellectually — the cognitive dimension of understanding; the stomach serves only as a way-station, not a defiler) — kai eis aphedrōna ekballetai (and is cast out into the latrine — aphedrōn: privy, toilet — a frank anatomical term): the digestive argument is deliberately physical; food passes through and out of the body without leaving a lasting moral trace. The moral life is not determined by what the body processes.
18: ta de ekporeuomena ek tou stomatos ek tēs kardias exerchetai — but what comes out of the mouth comes from the heart (exerchetai present: comes out, flows out — a continuous outward flow from the inner source; kardia: the heart as the center of will, desire, intention, and moral character in Jewish anthropology) — kakeina koinoi ton anthrōpon (and those things defile the person — kakeina: and those things; the pronoun refers to the mouth's outputs that originate in the heart): the principle reverses the Pharisaic framework — purity is an inside-out matter, not an outside-in ritual.
19: ek gar tēs kardias exerchontai dialogismoi ponēroi — for out of the heart come evil deliberations/thoughts (dialogismoi ponēroi: evil deliberations — dialogismos: a reasoning, calculation, deliberate thought; not passing emotions but considered choices that lead to action) — the list that follows covers the second table of the Decalogue plus extensions: phonoi (murders), moicheiai (adulteries), porneiai (sexual immoralities), klopai (thefts), pseudomarturiai (false testimonies), blasphēmiai (slanders/blasphemies): six plural nouns covering the full range of interpersonal and religious violations. The heart is the origin of all of these, not external contamination.
20: tauta estin ta koinounta ton anthrōpon — these are the things that defile the person — to de aniptois chersin phagein ou koinoi ton anthrōpon (but eating with unwashed hands does not defile the person): the return to the original charge (Matthew 15:2) completes the argument. The purity controversy is resolved not by lowering the standard of purity but by relocating it: unwashed-hands eating is a non-issue; speaking from an evil heart is the real defilement. The verdict is direct and unambiguous — "does not defile" (ou koinoi).
21: kai exelthōn ekeithen ho Iēsous anechōrēsen eis ta merē Tyrou kai Sidōnos — going out from there Jesus withdrew to the regions of Tyre and Sidon (anechōrēsen: withdrew — the deliberate-withdrawal verb; ta merē Tyrou kai Sidōnos: the regions of Tyre and Sidon — the non-Jewish northwest coastal territories): the Canaanite woman encounter happens in Gentile territory — a geographical shift that frames her faith as exceptional precisely because she is outside the covenant. The cities Jesus denounced in Matthew 11:21-22 for not repenting are now the setting for a faith-encounter.
22: kai idou gynē Chananaios apo tōn horiōn ekeinōn exelthousa ekrazen — and behold, a Canaanite woman from those regions came out and cried (Chananaios: Canaanite — an archaic LXX ethnic label for the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the land; Mark 7:26 uses "Syro-Phoenician" as the contemporary geographic term. Matthew's use of the archaic name invokes the covenant-history of Canaan and Israel) — kyrie huie Dauid eleēson me (Lord, Son of David, have mercy on me — the Davidic-Messiah address from a Canaanite, not an Israelite; she knows the messianic title even outside the covenant community).
23: ho de ouk apekrithē autē logon — but he did not answer her a word (ouk apekrithē autē logon: the idiom is literally "did not answer her a word" — a complete, deliberate silence): the silence is not indifference but a test. The disciples' response — apoluson autēn hoti krazei opisthen hēmōn (send her away, for she keeps crying after us) — is ambiguous: they may want her dismissed or want Jesus to grant her request so she stops following. Either way, their motive is self-interest, not compassion.
24: ouk apestellēn ei mē eis ta probata ta apolōlota oikou Israēl — I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel (ouk...ei mē: not...except, only; the exclusive limitation is stated directly; ta probata ta apolōlota: the lost sheep — perfect passive participle of apollymi: the sheep in a state of lostness; echoing 10:6 where the disciples were sent to the same lost sheep) — the limitation is not permanent (28:19 gives the universal commission) but is the scope of the earthly mission to Israel. The Canaanite woman's faith will overcome this territorial limitation.
25: hē de elthousa prosekynei autō legousa kyrie boēthei moi — but she came and was worshipping him saying: Lord, help me (prosekynei imperfect: was continuously worshipping, kneeling — an ongoing posture of petition; boēthei present imperative of boētheō: help, assist — boē + theō: run to a cry): the woman persists despite the silence and the limitation-statement. Her address shifts from "Son of David" (messianic title) to "Lord" (kyrie, the personal address of relationship), marking an escalation of intimacy in her appeal.
26: ouk estin kalon labein ton arton tōn teknōn kai balein tois kynarois — it is not good to take the children's bread and throw it to the little dogs (kalon: good, appropriate, fitting; teknōn: children, the covenant people; kynarois: diminutive — little dogs, puppies, house dogs — not the derogatory kyōn of 7:6 but the domestic diminutive, softening the comparison): the image sets up the woman's response. The diminutive kynarion leaves space for the response of Matthew 15:27 — household dogs eat under the table alongside the children.
27: hē de eipen nai kyrie kai gar ta kynarion esthiei apo tōn psichion — but she said: yes Lord, for even the little dogs eat from the crumbs (nai: yes — she accepts the comparison without resentment; kai gar: for even, precisely because of this: the particle combination accepts and extends the argument) — tōn piptontōn apo tēs trapezēs tōn kyriōn autōn (that fall from the table of their masters — trapezēs tōn kyriōn autōn: the masters' table): she takes the image and turns it: if she is a household dog, then the crumbs of the children's meal are still a share in it. Her argument is not a claim of equality but of proximity.
28: tote apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen autē ō gynai megalē sou hē pistis — then answering Jesus said to her: O woman, your faith is great (megalē sou hē pistis: your faith is great — megalē, the adjective of greatness; the contrast with the disciples' oligopistia is sharp: the Canaanite woman receives the superlative-positive while the disciples repeatedly receive the negative) — genēthētō soi hōs theleis (let it be done for you as you wish — genēthētō aorist passive imperative: let it become, let it happen — the same form as "your will be done" in the Lord's Prayer). Kai iathē hē thygatēr autēs apo tēs hōras ekeinēs — her daughter was healed from that hour.
29: kai metabas ekeithen ho Iēsous ēlthen para tēn thalassan tēs Galilaias — moving on from there Jesus came alongside the Sea of Galilee (metabas aorist participle of metabainō: having moved on from one place to another; the verb marks deliberate geographical transition) — kai anabas eis to oros ekathēto ekei (and going up on the mountain he was sitting there — ekathēto imperfect: was sitting in the teaching position): the mountainside setting for the healing summary deliberately echoes the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1). Jesus sits in the position of the authoritative teacher; the healings that follow are the teacher's actions, not merely miracles.
30: kai prosēlthon autō ochloi polloi echontes meth heautōn chōlous typhlous — and large crowds came to him having with them lame, blind — the list continues: kyllous (crippled), kōphous (mute/deaf), and many others. Kai erripsan autous para tous podas autou (and cast them at his feet — erripsan aorist of rhiptō: to throw down, cast at the feet — the same verb used in 9:36 for the crowds being "cast down" like helpless sheep): the casting at Jesus's feet is a formal act of bringing helpless people to the healer's authority. Kai etherapeuse autous — and he healed them.
31: hōste tous ochlous thaumasai bleponton kōphous lalountas — so that the crowds marveled, seeing mute people speaking — the healing list includes kyllous hygieis (crippled made sound), chōlous peripatountas (lame walking), typhlous blepontas (blind seeing): the same categories as the messianic answer to John (Matthew 11:5). Kai edoxasan ton theon Israēl (and they glorified the God of Israel — Israēl: the God of Israel specifically, which suggests these crowds may be Gentiles marveling at Israel's God): the praise identifies the source of the healings as the covenant God.
32: ho de Iēsous proskalesamenos tous mathētas autou eipen splanchnizomai epi ton ochlon — calling his disciples to him Jesus said: I have compassion for the crowd (splanchnizomai: the visceral compassion verb, used also in 9:36 and Matthew 14:14; here Jesus speaks the compassion explicitly rather than the narrator reporting it) — hēmerai treis prosmenousin moi kai ouk echousin ti phagōsin (they have remained with me three days and have nothing to eat): the three-day duration suggests a sustained teaching event, not a single encounter. Kai apolusai autous nēsteis ou thelō (and I do not want to send them away hungry — ou thelō: I am not willing; the verb of will).
33: kai legousin autō hoi mathētai pothen hēmin en erēmia artoi tosoutoi — the disciples say to him: where in a deserted place do we have enough bread for such a crowd — the disciples' question is remarkable given that they participated in the first feeding. Their failure to connect the two situations is not stupidity but a pattern of forgetting that Jesus will explicitly rebuke in Matthew 16:9-10. Hōste chortasai ochlon tosouton (so as to satisfy such a crowd — chortasai: to fill to satisfaction, the same verb as Matthew 14:20): the question expresses genuine inadequacy, not unbelief.
34: kai legei autois ho Iēsous posous artous echete — Jesus says to them: how many loaves do you have? (posous: how many — the question is identical to Mark 8:5): hoi de eipan hepta kai oligia ichthydia (they said: seven, and a few small fish — oligia ichthydia: a few small fish; the diminutive ichthydia emphasizes their smallness): the inventory is different from the first feeding (five loaves + two fish) but equally inadequate for the crowd. The specific number seven will be recalled in Matthew 16:10.
35: kai parangeilas tō ochlō anapesein epi tēn gēn — commanding the crowd to recline on the ground (parangeilas aorist participle of parangellō: to pass along an order, to command authoritatively): the command to sit is a direct action by Jesus, identical in structure to Matthew 14:19. The crowd's compliance before any food is visible is itself an act of trust. The reclining posture (anapesein) is the formal meal-posture, making the feeding a proper meal event.
36: kai labōn tous hepta artous kai tous ichthyas eucharistēsas eklasen — taking the seven loaves and the fish, giving thanks he broke them (eucharistēsas aorist participle of eucharistō: to give thanks — distinguished from Matthew 14:19's eulogēsen (blessed/invoked blessing): the first feeding uses eulogeō, the Jewish berakah form; the second uses eucharistō, the word that gives "Eucharist" its name) — kai edidou tois mathētais hoi de mathētai tois ochlois (and he was giving to the disciples, and the disciples to the crowds): the distribution chain is identical to Matthew 14.
37: kai ephagon pantes kai echortasthēsan — they all ate and were satisfied (echortasthēsan: the satisfaction-verb, cf. Matthew 14:20) — kai to perisseuon tōn klasmatōn ēran hepta spyridas plēreis (and they picked up the leftover fragments, seven baskets full — spyridas: large wicker hampers, a different word from the kophinoi of 14:20 which were personal Jewish wicker baskets; spyris is a larger carrying-basket): the different basket-type is not incidental — Jesus specifically references both numbers and basket-types in Matthew 16:9-10, indicating the distinction matters for interpretation.
38: hoi de esthiontes ēsan tetrakischilioi andres — those eating were four thousand men (tetrakischilioi: four thousand — a round number; the distinction between the first (five thousand) and second (four thousand) feedings suggests two separate events, not two versions of one) — chōris gynaikōn kai paidiōn (besides women and children — the same qualifier as Matthew 14:21): Matthew's consistent addition of women and children to the male count indicates the total was substantially more than four thousand.
39: kai apolusas tous ochlous enebē eis to ploion kai ēlthen eis ta horia Magadan — dismissing the crowds he got into the boat and came to the region of Magadan (apolusas: having dismissed; Magadan: an otherwise unattested place name; Mark 8:10 has Dalmanutha; both may be variant names for the same location on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee): the geographical transition moves from the mountain-feeding to the sign-demand of Matthew 16:1.
